children.name: Buceros
children.description: Large, spectacular hornbills with enormous casques atop the bill, which in some species are solid and used for aerial combat. The genus includes the most iconic Asian hornbills, revered in many cultures as symbols of majesty and good fortune. Their wingbeats produce a distinctive whooshing sound audible from a distance.

children.name: Buceros bicornis
children.description: One of the largest and most recognisable hornbills, found in forests of the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It has a massive yellow-and-black casque, a black band across the white neck, and a striking white and black body. In many tribal cultures the casque is used in ornaments and the bird is the subject of numerous myths.

children.name: Buceros rhinoceros
children.description: Named for the prominent upturned casque that resembles a rhinoceros horn, this is one of the largest hornbills of Southeast Asian rainforests. It has a striking black body, white thighs and vent, and a bright yellow-orange bill and casque with red highlights. It is the state bird of the Malaysian state of Sarawak.

children.name: Rhinoplax
children.description: A monotypic genus of endangered hornbill with a solid, front-heavy casque made of keratin, unlike the hollow casques of most other hornbills. The Helmeted Hornbill is unique for the intense red colour of the casque, which is produced by the bird preening its uropygial gland and rubbing the secretion into the casque.

children.name: Rhinoplax vigil
children.description: A critically endangered hornbill of the lowland rainforests of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. It has a solid casque of 'ivory' that has been carved into ornaments for centuries, driving massive poaching pressure. Its loud, laughing call carries through the forest and is distinctive among the hornbill chorus.

children.name: Tockus
children.description: Small to medium-sized hornbills of African savannas and woodlands, typically with a long, decurved bill and a relatively small casque. They are the most terrestrial of the arboreal hornbills, often foraging on the ground for insects and small vertebrates.

children.name: Tockus leucomelas
children.description: A familiar hornbill of southern African savannas and dry woodlands, easily recognised by its long yellow bill. It has a white belly, spotted black-and-white wings, and a diagnostic dark eyestripe. It is featured in the popular documentary series Meerkat Manor, where it was immortally named 'Tiny the Hornbill'.

children.name: Lophoceros
children.description: African hornbills characterised by a relatively small casque and a distinctive pale bill often with a dark tip or stripe. They are adaptable birds of savanna, woodland, and forest edge, and are among the most frequently encountered hornbills across Africa.

children.name: Lophoceros nasutus
children.description: Widespread across sub-Saharan Africa in savanna and open woodland. It has a uniform grey-brown body, a pale bill with a dark stripe on the upper mandible, and a white vent. It often perches conspicuously on dead branches, uttering a thin, piping whistled call.

children.name: Anthracoceros
children.description: Asian hornbills with glossy black plumage and large, heavy bills typically in contrasting shades of ivory, yellow and black. They are forest-dwellers that feed mainly on fruit but also take small animals. Their loud, raucous calls are a prominent sound in tropical Asian forests.

children.name: Anthracoceros albirostris
children.description: A small, common hornbill found from the Indian subcontinent through Southeast Asia to Borneo and the Philippines. It has glossy black plumage with a white belly and face, and a large yellow-white bill with a black patch on the side. It adapts well to secondary forests and urban areas, even nesting in cavities in buildings.

children.name: Rhyticeros
children.description: Large, predominantly frugivorous hornbills of Southeast Asian forests, characterised by a wrinkled or folded casque on the upper bill. Males have bright yellow or orange facial and throat skin, while females have blue or green skin. They are important seed dispersers for many rainforest trees.

children.name: Rhyticeros undulatus
children.description: A large hornbill of the Asian mainland and Greater Sundas, ranging from northeast India through Southeast Asia. The male has a creamy-white head and neck, a bright yellow gular pouch, and a low wrinkled casque atop the bill. It gathers in large roosting flocks that can number over a hundred birds.
